fact: January 3 is exactly 3 days into the new year, which means Earth has already traveled about 1.8 million miles through space since New Year's Day!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many evergreen trees start dropping their oldest needles - creating natural confetti on snowy ground.
January 3 falls during the darkest weeks of winter in northern countries, when daylight typically lasts only 8-9 hours.
Penguins in Antarctica are having their summer right now - January 3 means warm weather and 20+ hours of sunlight at the South Pole!
The sunrise on January 3 happens about 1 minute later than it did on January 1 in most places, even though days are getting longer.
Bears in hibernation have been asleep for about a month by now, and their heart rate has slowed to just 8 beats per minute - that's slower than a sleeping human!
January 3 is in the middle of Capricorn season, making it one of the most 'earthy' and practical days of the year according to astrology.
This is prime time for finding 'frost flowers' - weird ice formations that look like cotton candy growing out of frozen ground in cold climates.
In northern lakes and ponds, ice is getting thick enough for small animals to walk on, but fish below are swimming in water that's actually warmer than the air above.
The Earth is at its closest point to the Sun (called perihelion) right around January 3 - we're about 3 million miles closer than we'll be in July!
Deciduous trees are in their deepest sleep mode now, with sap moving so slowly it would take weeks to fill a teaspoon.
January 3 sunsets in snowy places create the most intense pink and purple sky colors of the year because ice crystals act like tiny prisms in the air.
